steps.title: Identify what the evidence must establish
steps.content: The student’s claim has two required elements: (1) Kelly observed women in his family, and (2) those observations specifically involved buttons used decoratively. Evidence that omits either element does not support the claim.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the correct one
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Although some of the assortments of buttons appear to be mismatched pieces scattered randomly throughout Kelly's dresses, his most famous designs feature carefully crafted patterns of matching buttons.": describes Kelly’s finished designs; no family member and no childhood observation are mentioned. ✗
  • Choice B — "Many of Kelly's contemporaries were inspired by his designs to incorporate buttons, as well as zippers and snaps, as decorative items in their work.": reverses causation — Kelly influenced others; it does not show Kelly being influenced by his family. ✗
  • Choice C — "Kelly's grandmother, who would repair clothing when he was a child, frequently added mismatched buttons to the clothes to draw attention away from any flaws in the garments.": a family woman (grandmother) using buttons decoratively during Kelly’s childhood — both required elements are present. ✓
  • Choice D — "Kelly was destined to be a designer from a young age: he learned how to sew clothing from his aunt Bertha, and his love of drawing was developed by his mother.": mentions family women influencing Kelly’s general skills, but says nothing about buttons. ✗
That matches Choice C — "Kelly's grandmother, who would repair clothing when he was a child, frequently added mismatched buttons to the clothes to draw attention away from any flaws in the garments.".
